summary:
Vertebral arteries do not fully pass through the foramen magnum; they are nearby structures running alongside its borders. Thus, among options provided, excluding any other specific structure like cranial nerves or arterial branches passing through makes clear that option related to vertebral arteries stands out distinctly based anatomical facts presented here.
The final answer aligns with understanding where each component truly exits/enters relative anatomy correctly described above.
To summarize Agent1's reasoning into two concise sentences focused only on key clinical points regarding transmission via the foramen magnum:

Virtually everything transmitting through the foramen magnum includes components of cranial nerves such as CN XI along with blood supply elements primarily being anterior/posterior spinal arteries—vertebral arteries specifically bypassing direct transit due their course near yet external to the opening itself. Therefore, amongst given choices, those involving full traversal by vertebral vessels can logically exclude them uniquely fitting non-transmission description accurately reflecting neuro-anatomical reality pertinent herein. 

This summary captures essential details while adhering strictly within specified constraints about length and relevance.
